content:
https://cft.vanderbilt.edu/guides-sub-pages/developing-and-writing-a-diversity-statement/


I check those “dreaded” boxes: privileged, check; white, check; male, check. I grew up in the only red borough of New York City: Staten Island — a product of white flight from Brooklyn—and lived a very comfortable life (monetarily not emotionally). It wasn’t until High School, a Catholic high school, that I shared a classroom with an African American student for the first time. This is to say that in my own personal largely homogenous world, I was the other because I wasn’t the normative white male. I was less masculine, more feminine, I read instead of taking to any sport, and I was quite overweight and only gaining progressively so the older I got. Nevertheless, I thought people who spoke broken English were less intelligent, and I twas raised to be cautious of people of color were dangerous. What an idiot I was, and what a ton of learning I had to do.

My undergraduate school, College of Staten Island, has only recently become diverse as it opened up a set of dorms which invited a diverse group of young adults from across the five boroughs. So I count my blessings that I have left my tiny little bubble, and moved across the country to one of the most diverse states, Florida. At Florida Atlantic University, I was pleasantly surprised to experience a shift in the diversity of the student body. FAU values differences and promotes equality and created awareness and new perspectives of traditionally marginalized groups. It opens up the peers to cultural cross pollination. One of my most instrumental classroom practices in getting the students to get to know one another and warmed up to be comfortable speaking in front of the other, is I ask them personal questions about themselves (cause who doesn’t love to talk about themself?). Going through grad school made me realize how not diverse my background was.
